writ.title: Add a multiplySafely utility to nexus-core
writ.body:
# Add a multiplySafely utility to nexus-core

Add a TypeScript function `multiplySafely(a: number, b: number):
number | null` that returns `null` when the multiplication would
overflow `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER`, and otherwise returns
`a * b`.

## Acceptance criteria

- Function lives in
  `packages/framework/core/src/util/numeric.ts` (create the
  file and the `util/` directory if they don't exist).
- Function exported from `packages/framework/core/src/util/numeric.ts`.
- One unit test file at
  `packages/framework/core/src/util/numeric.test.ts` covering:
  a typical successful multiplication, an overflow case, and
  the boundary case where the result is exactly
  `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER`.
- Tests pass under
  `pnpm --filter @shardworks/nexus-core test`.
- Build passes under
  `pnpm --filter @shardworks/nexus-core build`.

## Context

This is a small bounded task chosen for the Laboratory
apparatus's first real implementer-driven trial run (X016 phase
2b). The trial codex is the nexus framework monorepo, so the
file targets the smallest, dependency-light package
(`@shardworks/nexus-core`) and the build/test commands are
package-scoped via pnpm filters to keep the implementer out of
the recursive `pnpm -r` path.

The brief is small enough to complete quickly in an implementer
session and large enough to produce a real animator session
record + at least one codex commit. Phase 2c will extend this
into the actual orientation-suppression A/B (baseline vs
strong-prompt brief variants).

## Notes for the implementer

- The package's existing tests use Node's built-in test runner
  (`node --test`) — see `id.test.ts` and `pid-helpers.test.ts`
  in the same `src/` directory for the test style.
- The package has zero runtime dependencies; do not add any.
- No need to update `index.ts` exports unless you want
  `multiplySafely` available as part of the public SDK surface
  — the brief only requires the function be exported from its
  own file.

yields.output:
Done. Here's a summary of what was implemented:

## Implementation

**`packages/framework/core/src/util/numeric.ts`** (new file)
- `multiplySafely(a, b)` computes `a * b` and returns `null` if the absolute value of the product exceeds `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER`, otherwise returns the product.

**`packages/framework/core/src/util/numeric.test.ts`** (new file)
Three tests following the repo's `node:test` style:
1. **Typical multiplication** — `6 * 7 = 42` → returns `42`
2. **Overflow case** — `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER * 2` → returns `null`
3. **Exact boundary** — `1 * Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER` → returns `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER`

**Verification:**
- ✅ All 42 tests pass (`pnpm --filter @shardworks/nexus-core test`)
- ✅ Build succeeds (`pnpm --filter @shardworks/nexus-core build`)
- ✅ Committed as `7b984d6`

writ.title: Add a multiplySafely utility to nexus-core
writ.body:
# Add a multiplySafely utility to nexus-core

Add a TypeScript function `multiplySafely(a: number, b: number):
number | null` that returns `null` when the multiplication would
overflow `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER`, and otherwise returns
`a * b`.

## Acceptance criteria

- Function lives in
  `packages/framework/core/src/util/numeric.ts` (create the
  file and the `util/` directory if they don't exist).
- Function exported from `packages/framework/core/src/util/numeric.ts`.
- One unit test file at
  `packages/framework/core/src/util/numeric.test.ts` covering:
  a typical successful multiplication, an overflow case, and
  the boundary case where the result is exactly
  `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER`.
- Tests pass under
  `pnpm --filter @shardworks/nexus-core test`.
- Build passes under
  `pnpm --filter @shardworks/nexus-core build`.

## Context

This is a small bounded task chosen for the Laboratory
apparatus's first real implementer-driven trial run (X016 phase
2b). The trial codex is the nexus framework monorepo, so the
file targets the smallest, dependency-light package
(`@shardworks/nexus-core`) and the build/test commands are
package-scoped via pnpm filters to keep the implementer out of
the recursive `pnpm -r` path.

The brief is small enough to complete quickly in an implementer
session and large enough to produce a real animator session
record + at least one codex commit. Phase 2c will extend this
into the actual orientation-suppression A/B (baseline vs
strong-prompt brief variants).

## Notes for the implementer

- The package's existing tests use Node's built-in test runner
  (`node --test`) — see `id.test.ts` and `pid-helpers.test.ts`
  in the same `src/` directory for the test style.
- The package has zero runtime dependencies; do not add any.
- No need to update `index.ts` exports unless you want
  `multiplySafely` available as part of the public SDK surface
  — the brief only requires the function be exported from its
  own file.

yields.findings:
### Overall: PASS

### Completeness

All spec requirements are addressed:

- `multiplySafely(a: number, b: number): number | null` implemented at `packages/framework/core/src/util/numeric.ts`
- Function is exported from that file
- Unit test file at `packages/framework/core/src/util/numeric.test.ts` covers all three required cases: typical multiplication, overflow, and exact boundary
- Tests pass (42 pass, 0 fail)
- Build passes

### Correctness

No bugs or logic errors. The implementation computes the product first, then checks `Math.abs(product) > Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER`, which correctly handles both positive and negative products. The boundary case (`result === Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER`) correctly returns the product rather than null since the check is strictly greater-than.

One subtle note: the spec says "overflow `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER`" without mentioning negative overflow, and the implementation uses `Math.abs()` to also guard against products below `-Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER`. This is a reasonable and defensively correct interpretation — it's not a bug.

### Quality

- Test style matches existing tests in the package (Node built-in `node:test` runner, `node:assert/strict`, `describe`/`it` structure)
- The docblock comment is accurate and useful — it documents a non-obvious invariant (the safe-integer bound check), which is the right threshold for a comment per the project's standards
- No new dependencies introduced
- Test coverage is appropriate: exactly the three cases required by the spec, no padding

### Required Changes

None.
